texts.facebook:
8% of subscribers are quietly walking out the door every month. 📉

Max storms into the room with a chart and zero patience: "We can't call 200,000 customers. Give me a list of who's leaving."

Lena's already thinking in code: binary classification, historical labels, probabilities the retention team can rank and act on.

This is Module 5 of ML Career Simulator: you're the Junior ML Engineer who has to turn panic into a churn model using logistic regression — and explain why the output is a probability, not a guess.

Step into the seat and solve it yourself: https://ml-simulator-app-production.up.railway.app/en.html?utm_source=facebook&utm_medium=social&utm_campaign=organic&utm_content=2026-07-21-story-60aed3
texts.linkedin:
"We can't call 200,000 customers. Give me a list of who's leaving in 30 days."

That's how the emergency sync starts in Module 5 of ML Career Simulator.

Max, the stakeholder, has a real budget problem: retaining a customer costs 5x less than acquiring one, but the retention team can't reach everyone. He needs priorities, not a spreadsheet of hunches.

Lena reframes it as a binary classification problem — historical labels, engineered features (activity, payments, tickets, tenure), and a model that outputs something retention can actually use: a probability of churn per customer.

You're the Junior ML Engineer in the room. Your job:

- Understand why linear regression breaks down here
- Wrap the math in a sigmoid to get real probabilities
- Train a logistic regression model with sklearn
- Deliver a ranked list the business can act on

This is one of 20 business cases inside ML Career Simulator, a gamified course where you grow from Junior to Middle ML Engineer at a fictional company, Datacore — by actually doing the work.

Try the churn case yourself: https://ml-simulator-app-production.up.railway.app/en.html?utm_source=linkedin&utm_medium=social&utm_campaign=organic&utm_content=2026-07-21-story-60aed3

#MachineLearning #MLEngineer #DataScience #LogisticRegression #TechCareer
